**Vooruit** (Dutch pronunciation: [[voːrˈœyt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-vooruit.ogg "File:Nl-vooruit.ogg"), lit. 'Forward') is a [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") [political party in Belgium](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Belgium "List of political parties in Belgium").

The party was founded following the linguistic split of the unitary [Belgian Socialist Party](/wiki/Belgian_Socialist_Party "Belgian Socialist Party") in 1978, which also produced the Francophone [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(Belgium) "Socialist Party (Belgium)"). It was formerly known as the Socialist Party[[6]](#cite_note-6) (1978–2001: *Socialistische Partij*, **SP**) and subsequently "Socialist Party Alternative" (2001–2021: *Socialistische Partij Anders*, **SP.A**) until 2021, when its current name was adopted.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

From December 2011 to September 2014, the party was part of the [Di Rupo Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_Government "Di Rupo Government"), along with its Francophone counterpart. In 2020, it re-entered federal government as part of the [De Croo Government](/wiki/De_Croo_Government "De Croo Government"). The party has been a part of the [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government") several times.

The party was the big winner in the [2003 election](/wiki/2003_Belgian_federal_election "2003 Belgian federal election"), running on the [SP.A–Spirit](/wiki/SP.A%E2%80%93Spirit "SP.A–Spirit") joint list ([cartel](/wiki/Kartel_(electoral_alliance) "Kartel (electoral alliance)")) with the [social-liberal](/wiki/Social_liberalism "Social liberalism") party [Spirit](/wiki/Social_Liberal_Party_(Belgium) "Social Liberal Party (Belgium)"). Their share of the vote went up from 9% (of the total Belgian vote) to almost 15%, a second place in the number of votes. The main victim of this resurgence was the [Green!](/wiki/Green! "Green!") party (formerly known as *Agalev*). SP.A was part of the "[purple](/wiki/Purple_(government) "Purple (government)")" federal coalitions of Prime Minister [Guy Verhofstadt](/wiki/Guy_Verhofstadt "Guy Verhofstadt") from 12 July 1999 until 10 June 2007, which contained both the Flemish and Francophone liberal and social-democratic parties.

In 2004, the SP.A along with its partner Spirit lost the elections for the [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament"). Although they won more seats in comparison to the Flemish elections of 1999, their percentage of the vote compared to the successful 2003 federal elections was considerably down. The reputation of then party leader [Steve Stevaert](/wiki/Steve_Stevaert "Steve Stevaert") took a beating too.

The party was briefly led by [Caroline Gennez](/wiki/Caroline_Gennez "Caroline Gennez"), after former president [Steve Stevaert](/wiki/Steve_Stevaert "Steve Stevaert") left to become governor of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)"). [Johan Vande Lanotte](/wiki/Johan_Vande_Lanotte "Johan Vande Lanotte"), who served as Minister of the Budget in the federal Government, was elected President and resigned as minister to become President on 17 October 2005. He resigned 11 June 2007, after SP.A–Spirit lost the [elections for the federal parliament](/wiki/2007_Belgian_federal_election "2007 Belgian federal election") of 10 June 2007.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In these [federal elections](/wiki/2007_Belgian_federal_election "2007 Belgian federal election"), the cartel won 14 out of 150 seats in the [Chamber of Representatives](/wiki/Belgian_Chamber_of_Representatives "Belgian Chamber of Representatives") and 4 out of 40 seats in the [Senate](/wiki/Belgian_Senate "Belgian Senate"). Afterwards, Caroline Gennez was elected President by the party members.

As of May 2009, SP.A was in opposition in federal politics. Unlike its Francophone counterpart, the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Parti_Socialiste_(Belgium) "Parti Socialiste (Belgium)") (PS), SP.A was not a participant in the [Leterme II Government](/wiki/Leterme_II_Government "Leterme II Government").

In January 2009, the party had apparently changed its name to Socialists and Progressive Differently (Dutch: *Socialisten en Progressieven Anders*). This name change was retracted and the party baseline was changed from Social Progressive Alternative (Dutch: *Sociaal Progressief Alternatief*) to Socialists and Progressive Differently (Dutch: *Socialisten en Progressieven Anders*).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In the [2010 federal election](/wiki/2010_Belgian_federal_election "2010 Belgian federal election"), SP.A won 13 seats with 9% of the overall vote. The party was a member of the [Di Rupo Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_Government "Di Rupo Government") formed on 6 December 2011, until the elections in 2014. In the elections for the Chamber of Representatives on 25 May 2014, SP.A scored again 9% and received 13 seats, in contrast to their francophone Socialist Party counterparts, who lost 3 seats and whose share of the vote decreased by 2%. In the [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament"), SP.A have 18 representatives, deriving from around 14% of the vote—this is a small reduction on the 2009 parliament, where SP.A had 19 seats, deriving from 15% of the popular vote. From 2009–2014, SP.A participated in the [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government"), in an uneasy coalition with the CD & V and the N-VA. From 2014 onwards, SP.A formed part of the opposition in Flanders, as the regional government reflected the Flemish component of the federal administration, consisting of coalition of the Open-VLD, CD & V and the N-VA.

Leaders and representatives at a climate march, October 2021

In January 2018, the party advocated for a "new socialism" and a "new equality".[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) In September 2020, party leader Conner Rousseau announced a renaming of the party to *Vooruit* ("Forward").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The new name was made official on 21 March 2021.

## Political views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vooruit_(political_party)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Political views")]

Vooruit is a [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics")[[13]](#cite_note-Colomer2008-13) Flemish [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") party.[[14]](#cite_note-Nordsieck-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Slomp2011-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Almeida-16)[[17]](#cite_note-AnttiroikoMälkiä2007-17)[[18]](#cite_note-MuddeKaltwasser2012-18) [Equal opportunities](/wiki/Equal_opportunities "Equal opportunities") and [solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity "Solidarity") are central to the party. The party strives to ensure that those who earn the most or own the greatest wealth also pay the highest taxes so that the government can meet the needs of people who are less fortunate. Vooruit focuses on protecting the [purchasing power](/wiki/Purchasing_power "Purchasing power") of ordinary citizens. It opposes cuts in [social security](/wiki/Social_security "Social security") and advocates investment, especially in [education](/wiki/Education "Education") and [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care").[[19]](#cite_note-vrt20240322-19)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]
